chapters.title: Use a Shorter Horizon to Create Urgency

summaryBullets: Treating twelve weeks as a real execution horizon instead of waiting on an annual calendar creates clearer judgment.
Shorter cycles make urgency visible and make drift harder to ignore.
This matters because people execute more honestly when time pressure is concrete and close.
A common mistake is hiding behind a distant annual plan that always feels recoverable.
The pattern becomes easier to see when the goal still sounds important but execution keeps slipping into later months.
A practical starting move is to set a twelve week target, score progress every week, and name the work that truly matters now.
keyTakeaways: Treating twelve weeks as a real execution horizon instead of waiting on an annual calendar creates clearer judgment. This perspective helps people make clearer decisions and follow through with more discipline when stakes rise.

chapters.title: Connect Vision to the Weekly Plan

summaryBullets: Linking long term vision to a weekly plan that can actually be executed creates clearer judgment.
Big goals only move when daily and weekly actions point in the same direction.
This matters because clarity improves when vision and execution stop living in separate systems.
A common mistake is writing inspiring goals without translating them into weekly commitments.
The pattern becomes easier to see when people know the aspiration but cannot say what this week is supposed to accomplish.
A practical starting move is to translate the goal into weekly actions, write the lead behaviors, and review whether the week matches the vision.
keyTakeaways: Linking long term vision to a weekly plan that can actually be executed creates clearer judgment. This perspective helps people make clearer decisions and follow through with more discipline when stakes rise.
practice: translate the goal into weekly actions
write the lead behaviors
review whether the week matches the vision
cut work that does not support the cycle
